rules.description: Right-to-know disclosure on data collected
Right to delete disclosure
Right to opt out of sale or sharing
Do Not Sell or Share My Personal Information link
Subject line cannot be deceptive
From header must identify sender
Postal address required in email
Opt-out within 10 business days
Fair-processing privacy notice meeting DIFC DPL Article 29
Registration with the DIFC Commissioner of Data Protection
Author byline on substantive content (Experience + Expertise)
Author credentials visible (Authoritativeness)
Consent that is freely given, specific, informed and revocable
Identity and contact of the data controller disclosed before collection
Purposes and legal basis of processing stated
Data subject rights (access, correction, erasure, restriction, withdraw) disclosed
US public-accommodation website with no evident accessibility conformance under ADA Title III
WCAG 2.1 AA accessibility statement
No false or misleading statements about the lawyer or services (ABA 7.1)
Commercial messages must be labelled if subscriber opted in for transactional only
Reviews or testimonials shown to US consumers without genuine-review safeguards under the FTC Consumer Review Rule
Sites with influencer, affiliate or AI content must disclose endorsements clearly.
"Do Not Sell or Share" / "Your Privacy Choices" opt-out link
Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal
State-compliant privacy notice (categories, purposes, rights, opt-out methods)
Virginia requires a controller privacy notice including access, deletion, portability rights.
GDPR-aligned privacy notice for ADGM controllers
Registration with the ADGM Commissioner + GDPR-aligned notice
DIFC data subject rights disclosed
Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)
Published content shows author / expertise signals (E-E-A-T)
Cross-border transfer of personal data outside the UAE disclosed
Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)
Past-results / testimonial disclaimer
"Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link
Endorsements, ambassador or affiliate content without a clear material-connection disclosure
Consumer rights (access, delete, correct, opt-out, appeal) + request method
Opt-in / limit for sensitive data (Nebraska, Maryland MODPA, others)
DPIA + appropriate-safeguards posture (ADGM)
Data Protection Officer where required + contact
Virginia consumer rights + mandatory appeal process

terms.da: Domain Authority: a 0 to 100 trust score based on the quality of sites linking to you. The higher it is, the easier everything else ranks.
terms.pa: Page Authority: the same trust idea as Domain Authority but for one specific page rather than the whole site.
terms.cls: Cumulative Layout Shift: how much your page jumps around while loading. Jumpy pages frustrate users and rank worse.
terms.csp: Content-Security-Policy: a security header that blocks malicious scripts. Security reviews check for it.
terms.geo: Generative Engine Optimisation: whether AI answer engines (ChatGPT, Gemini, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews) actually name and cite your business when a buyer asks them for a provider like you.
terms.lcp: Largest Contentful Paint: how long your main content takes to appear. Over 2.5 seconds and Google ranks you lower and visitors leave.
terms.nap: Name, Address, Phone. Search engines cross-check these to trust that your business is real and local. Inconsistent or missing NAP sinks local rankings.
terms.ccpa: California's privacy law, and the template for the roughly 20 US states that now have one. It gives consumers the right to opt out and to have data deleted.
terms.gdpr: The data-protection law for the UK and EU. Fines reach GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover, so gaps here are the most expensive on the page.
terms.hsts: A security header that stops your site being downgraded from secure HTTPS. Corporate buyers check for it, so its absence is an easy red flag.
terms.pecr: UK rules on cookies and electronic marketing. You must get clear consent before non-essential cookies or trackers fire.
terms.sameas: Schema links that connect your site to your verified profiles (LinkedIn, Companies House, Wikidata) so AI knows the business is really you.
terms.schema: Hidden code (structured data) that tells search engines and AI exactly who you are, what you offer and where. Without it they have to guess, and they guess wrong.
terms.e-e-a-t: Google's test of Experience, Expertise, Authoritativeness and Trust. It decides whether your content deserves to rank, and it matters most in law, health and finance.
terms.organic: The unpaid search results, as opposed to ads. Ranking here is the durable, compounding kind of visibility.
terms.uk gdpr: The UK version of the EU data-protection law, enforced by the ICO with fines up to GBP 17.5M or 4% of global turnover.
terms.llms.txt: A simple file that tells AI models what your site is about and what they may use. It is a new standard and most firms do not have one yet, so adding it is an easy edge.
terms.map pack: The three-business map result at the top of local searches. The top spots take the majority of local clicks.
terms.wikidata: Google's and AI's open knowledge base. An entry there makes you a recognised entity that answer engines can confidently cite.
terms.canonical: A line of code that tells Google which version of a page is the real one, so duplicate web addresses do not split your ranking power.
terms.ai crawler: The automated readers (GPTBot, ClaudeBot, PerplexityBot, Google-Extended) that AI answer engines send to read your site. Block them and those engines cannot cite you.
terms.local pack: The three businesses Google shows on a small map at the top of local searches. Most local clicks go to those three, so being outside it costs real enquiries.
terms.robots.txt: A file that tells web crawlers which parts of your site they may read. One wrong line here can make you invisible to Google or to AI engines.
terms.ai visibility: Whether the AI answer engines buyers now ask first will name and cite you, or only your competitors.
terms.share of voice: How often you appear versus your competitors when buyers search or ask AI in your category. Low share of voice means they are heard and you are not.
terms.structured data: Hidden code that spells out who you are and what you offer so Google and AI can read it precisely instead of guessing.
terms.domain authority: A 0 to 100 score of how much trust your site has earned from links on other websites. Higher means Google and AI engines are more likely to rank and cite you. It is the single biggest reason one site outranks another.
terms.meta description: The summary Google shows under your result. A good one lifts click-through; if it is missing, Google writes its own from random page text.

news_map.US_ADA: DOJ ADA Title III digital-accessibility rule finalised April 2024. 4,000+ web-accessibility lawsuits in 2024.
news_map.US_FTC: FTC (2026-05-21): FTC to Require Cox Media Group, Two Other Firms to Pay Nearly $1 Million to Settle Charges They Deceived Customers About “Active Listening” AI-Powered Marketing Service — $1M. Source: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-require-cox-media-group-two-other-firms-pay-nearly-1-million-settle-charges-they-deceived
news_map.US_CCPA: California CPPA brought 12 enforcement actions in 2024, largest fine $1.55M.
news_map.GOOGLE_EEAT: Google March 2024 core update emphasised E-E-A-T; sites without author bylines saw 31% traffic drop.
news_map.US_FTC_ENDORSE: FTC (2026-05-21): FTC to Require Cox Media Group, Two Other Firms to Pay Nearly $1 Million to Settle Charges They Deceived Customers About “Active Listening” AI-Powered Marketing Service — $1M. Source: https://www.ftc.gov/news-events/news/press-releases/2026/05/ftc-require-cox-media-group-two-other-firms-pay-nearly-1-million-settle-charges-they-deceived

pointers.fact: Right-to-know disclosure on data collected

bingo.fix: Tamazia implements and verifies "Right-to-know disclosure on data collected" on your site so it satisfies the CCPA as amended by CPRA.
bingo.problem: Right now: Right-to-know disclosure on data collected. Here it is on your site: “We do not sell, rent, distribute, or otherwise make, personal information commercially available to any third party.”.

pointers.evidence_quote: We do not sell, rent, distribute, or otherwise make, personal information commercially available to any third party.
pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Right-to-know disclosure on data collected" on your site so it satisfies the CCPA as amended by CPRA.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Right-to-know disclosure on data collected" on your site so it satisfies the CCPA as amended by CPRA.
pointers.layman_explanation: Right-to-know disclosure on data collected. This is a specific requirement of the CCPA as amended by CPRA, which the CPPA enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
pointers.enforcement_example: California Privacy Protection Agency and Attorney General: civil penalties of USD 2,500 per violation (USD 7,500 if intentional or involving minors); e.g. Sephora USD 1.2M settlement (2022).

pointers.fact: Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal

bingo.fix: Tamazia implements server- and client-side GPC detection wired to your opt-out.
bingo.problem: Right now: Honour the Global Privacy Control universal opt-out signal.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements server- and client-side GPC detection wired to your opt-out.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements server- and client-side GPC detection wired to your opt-out.
pointers.layman_explanation: Most US privacy states now require your site to detect and honour the Global Privacy Control browser signal for opting out of data sale/sharing. Your site shows no GPC handling, the exact failure the CA/CO/CT joint sweep is targeting.
pointers.enforcement_example: California, Colorado and Connecticut launched a joint 2025 sweep against businesses that ignore Global Privacy Control opt-outs; 11+ states now require honouring it.

pointers.fact: Your AI entity-readiness score is 57/100: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt.

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
bingo.problem: Right now: Your AI entity-readiness score is 57/100: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt. Here it is on your site: “no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt”.

pointers.evidence_quote: no Organization entity schema; no sameAs links to your authoritative profiles; no Wikidata knowledge-graph entry; no llms.txt
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds your machine-readable entity: full Organization schema with sameAs, a Wikidata entry, and an llms.txt, so the answer engines can identify and cite you.
pointers.layman_explanation: AI answer engines decide who to cite by first identifying you as a known, trusted entity. They read your Organization schema, the sameAs links that connect you to your verified profiles, and knowledge bases like Wikidata. You are missing 4 of these anchors, so to an AI engine you are an unknown string rather than a citable provider, and it names the firms it can identify instead.

pointers.fact: Asked who you are by name, a leading AI model has no reliable information about your firm.

bingo.fix: Tamazia builds the entity footprint (schema, Wikidata, authoritative mentions) that makes AI models actually know and describe you correctly.
bingo.problem: Right now: Asked who you are by name, a leading AI model has no reliable information about your firm. Here it is on your site: “model reply: "NO RELIABLE INFO."”.

pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the entity footprint (schema, Wikidata, authoritative mentions) that makes AI models actually know and describe you correctly.
pointers.layman_explanation: When a buyer asks an AI assistant about you by name, it cannot describe you, so it will not vouch for or recommend you, and when pushed it may invent details (a hallucination) that you cannot control. Competitors the model does know get described and recommended. Becoming a model-known entity is what fixes this.

pointers.fact: Registration with the ADGM Commissioner + GDPR-aligned notice

bingo.fix: Tamazia confirms ADGM registration and brings the notice to the ADGM standard.
bingo.problem: Right now: Registration with the ADGM Commissioner + GDPR-aligned notice.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia confirms ADGM registration and brings the notice to the ADGM standard.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia confirms ADGM registration and brings the notice to the ADGM standard.
pointers.layman_explanation: A controller established in ADGM must register and publish a GDPR-aligned notice. There is no evidence you have.
pointers.enforcement_example: ADGM Data Protection Regulations 2021 require registration and a GDPR-aligned privacy notice for controllers established in ADGM.

pointers.fact: DIFC data subject rights disclosed

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds a DIFC-compliant rights section and request workflow.
bingo.problem: Right now: DIFC data subject rights disclosed.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds a DIFC-compliant rights section and request workflow.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a DIFC-compliant rights section and request workflow.
pointers.layman_explanation: DIFC residents have GDPR-style data rights; failing to honour or disclose them is the DIFC Commissioner most-fined breach.
pointers.enforcement_example: DIFC DPL grants GDPR-style rights; breach of data-subject rights is the most heavily fined area (up to USD 100,000).

pointers.fact: Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)

bingo.fix: Tamazia implements and verifies "Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)" on your site so it satisfies Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content.
bingo.problem: Right now: Citations to authoritative sources (Trust).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia implements and verifies "Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)" on your site so it satisfies Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia implements and verifies "Citations to authoritative sources (Trust)" on your site so it satisfies Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content.
pointers.layman_explanation: Citations to authoritative sources (Trust). This is a specific requirement of Google E-E-A-T and Helpful Content, which Google Search enforces; for a firm selling trust, its absence on the live site is an avoidable compliance and credibility breach.
pointers.enforcement_example: Google Search ranking systems and manual actions: no statutory fine, but ranking suppression and loss of organic visibility; manual actions reported in Search Console.

pointers.fact: Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III)

bingo.fix: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
bingo.problem: Right now: Accessibility statement + WCAG 2.1 AA (ADA Title III).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia brings the site to WCAG 2.1 AA and adds an accessibility statement.
pointers.layman_explanation: ADA Title III expects WCAG 2.1 AA and an accessibility statement; missing.
pointers.enforcement_example: DOJ 2024 guidance treats WCAG 2.1 AA as the ADA standard; thousands of Title III suits yearly.

pointers.fact: "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
bingo.problem: Right now: "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the CPRA sensitive-data limit link.
pointers.layman_explanation: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where you use sensitive data; missing.
pointers.enforcement_example: CPRA requires a "Limit the Use of My Sensitive Personal Information" link where sensitive data drives inference.

pointers.fact: Consumer rights (access, delete, correct, opt-out, appeal) + request method

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds a consumer-rights request workflow meeting the state requirements.
bingo.problem: Right now: Consumer rights (access, delete, correct, opt-out, appeal) + request method.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds a consumer-rights request workflow meeting the state requirements.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds a consumer-rights request workflow meeting the state requirements.
pointers.layman_explanation: US state laws give consumers rights to access, delete, correct and opt out, plus an appeal route. Your site offers no clear way to exercise them.
pointers.enforcement_example: State laws grant access, deletion, correction, opt-out and (in most) an appeal right; failure to provide a request method is finable.

pointers.fact: Opt-in / limit for sensitive data (Nebraska, Maryland MODPA, others)

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds sensitive-data opt-in and a data-minimisation posture.
bingo.problem: Right now: Opt-in / limit for sensitive data (Nebraska, Maryland MODPA, others).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds sensitive-data opt-in and a data-minimisation posture.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds sensitive-data opt-in and a data-minimisation posture.
pointers.layman_explanation: If you process sensitive data (health, biometrics, precise location), states like Nebraska and Maryland require opt-in and strict necessity. Your site has no such control.
pointers.enforcement_example: Nebraska requires opt-in for sensitive data; Maryland MODPA (Oct 2025) adds strict data-minimisation and expansive sensitive-data definitions.

pointers.fact: DPIA + appropriate-safeguards posture (ADGM)

bingo.fix: Tamazia documents ADGM DPIAs and safeguards.
bingo.problem: Right now: DPIA + appropriate-safeguards posture (ADGM).

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia documents ADGM DPIAs and safeguards.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia documents ADGM DPIAs and safeguards.
pointers.layman_explanation: ADGM controllers must run DPIAs for high-risk processing; not evident.
pointers.enforcement_example: ADGM DP Regs require DPIAs for high-risk processing and appropriate safeguards.

pointers.fact: Virginia consumer rights + mandatory appeal process

bingo.fix: Tamazia adds the VCDPA rights and appeal process.
bingo.problem: Right now: Virginia consumer rights + mandatory appeal process.

pointers.recommendation: Tamazia adds the VCDPA rights and appeal process.
pointers.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia adds the VCDPA rights and appeal process.
pointers.layman_explanation: Virginia VCDPA grants rights plus a mandatory appeal process; your notice omits the appeal route.
pointers.enforcement_example: Virginia VCDPA grants rights plus a mandatory appeal process; up to USD 7,500/violation.

llm.answer: Al Tamimi & Company, Baker McKenzie, Clifford Chance, Dentons, DLA Piper, Hadef & Partners, Hogan Lovells, White & Case

needs_review.fact: Your live site is behind a bot-challenge wall, so it could not be read live for on-page assessment.
needs_review.layman_explanation: A bot-protection wall (Cloudflare/Akamai-style) blocks automated reading of the live HTML. Compliance is still assessed from the public web archive; live on-page SEO signals could not be read.

needs_review.fact: For "best law firms near online" you are not visible in the top 100; lawyers.findlaw.com owns position #2.
needs_review.evidence: live SERP (free-serp) · query "best law firms near online"
needs_review.evidence_quote: live SERP: you not visible in the top 100 vs lawyers.findlaw.com #2
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia builds the page depth and authority to move "best law firms near online" into the top three, where the clicks and the AI citations are.
needs_review.layman_explanation: When your buyers search "best law firms near online", they find lawyers.findlaw.com, not you. Page two and below is effectively zero clicks, so this exact query hands its high-intent traffic to a competitor every day, and AI answers cite the top-ranked pages, so being invisible here also keeps you out of the AI results. The firms winning your buyer queries: lawyers.findlaw.com, justia.com, robertsonmoss.com.

needs_review.fact: Your Domain Authority is 25/100. Your top 3 competitors: lawyers.findlaw.com 41/100, justia.com 41/100, courtscast.com 30/100.
needs_review.evidence_quote: you 25/100 (global rank 15,027,218) vs lawyers.findlaw.com 41/100, justia.com 41/100, courtscast.com 30/100
needs_review.tamazia_fix_short: Tamazia runs the digital-PR and authority-building programme (earned links, citations, entity coverage) that lifts your Domain Authority toward the firms outranking you.
needs_review.layman_explanation: Domain Authority is the backlink-trust score (here on a 0 to 100 scale) that Google and AI engines use to decide who to rank and cite. lawyers.findlaw.com carries 1.7 times of it than you, which is the structural reason these firms sit above you for your buyer queries and get cited in AI answers while you do not. Closing a rank gap without closing the authority gap rarely holds.

regimes.why: applies to every site that wants to rank in Google or be cited by AI answer engines, regardless of where it is registered

jurisdiction_statement.statement: tamimi is registered in UAE. It is therefore bound by the law of its home jurisdiction, not only the law of its country of registration. This audit applies the laws of every jurisdiction the site shows you operate in, and no others.
